_meta.description: Prompt variations from Appendix A.2. Three randomization axes: preamble × structure × variation. Full set on OSF prompts.yaml.

preambles: an editor of a top marketing journal such as the Journal of Consumer Research or the Journal of Marketing
a communication scientist researching the effects of linguistic framing on reader perception
a consumer psychology expert specializing in persuasive messaging
preambleNote: 9 total preambles; 3 shown here from Appendix A.2.1. All designed to encourage analytical thought.
hypothesisStructures: Hypothesis: ______ leads to {direction} engagement with a message.
Hypothesis: ______ makes people {direction} likely to engage with a message.
Hypothesis: ______ influences engagement with a message.
structureNote: 8 total structures; 3 shown here. {direction} filled with 'more/less' or 'increases/decreases' depending on which headline had higher CTR.

variations.name: Control
variations.description: Did not refer to any Upworthy headlines. Included to later assess whether hypotheses generated by GPT with access to the dataset differed from those generated without specific headlines.

variations.name: Examples
variations.description: Included examples of ideal hypotheses, such as 'taking photos with the intention to share will induce self-presentational concern and generate disutility, thus actually decreasing enjoyment of the current experience' and 'perception of moving at faster speed results in more abstract mental representation and choices consistent with desirability'.

variations.name: Known constructs
variations.description: Included known constructs sourced from the BU analysis, such as 'Reading Ease: Simpler and easier to read and understand' and 'Common Words: Contains more simple or common words'.

variationNote: 4 total variations (3 listed + no variation). Combined: 9 preambles × 8 structures × 4 variations = 288 prompt configurations.
qualityRating: Human raters (n = 79) believed the hypotheses were good quality.
